text: Your wife tells you over dinner she's been offered a senior position. The salary is higher than yours. She seems excited.

choices.text: "That's incredible — let me know how I can support you adjusting to it."
"Congrats. We should sit down and talk about how this changes things at home."
"Mashallah. Just take care of yourself — these roles eat people up."
"That's huge. Let's celebrate properly this weekend and work out the details later."

text: Your wife gets admission to a 3-year PhD in London. Partial scholarship. She'd be living abroad most of that time.

choices.text: "It's your dream. If you want it, we figure out the rest."
"This needs a real conversation. What about kids, family, distance?"
"I'm proud you got in. Let me think about how I'd manage things here."
"Whatever you decide, I'm with you — just don't let people pressure you into refusing."

text: Your wife wants to start a clothing business with her savings. It'll need 2 years to break even. Your cousins ask why she 'needs' to do this.

choices.text: "It's her venture, her money. We're happy with it."
"She's been planning this for months. We've thought it through."
"She finds it meaningful, and I'd rather her chase it than regret not trying."
"You know how women are when they have ideas — let her try, she'll figure it out."

text: Your wife is offered a 3-year posting in Dubai. To go, you'd leave your job, parents, and life here.

choices.text: "If this is what you want, I'll figure out my side."
"Let's both list what we'd be giving up, then decide together."
"I'll try to find work there. If I can't, we'll have to reconsider."
"You should take it. Three years isn't long — we can manage."

text: You can afford a house 45 minutes from your parents in a quiet suburb, OR a smaller apartment 5 minutes from them. Your wife prefers the suburb.

choices.text: "Let's get the suburb house — we need our space to build our own life."
"My parents are getting older. Living close helps, but let's talk."
"You'll be in the house more than me. Whatever feels right to you."
"The suburb is great, but I'd want to be at my parents' place often."

text: You and your wife live abroad. Your father is diagnosed with a chronic illness. Your siblings expect you to move back.

choices.text: "This is something I need to discuss with my wife. We'll decide together."
"I'll go for now — she can join when she's ready."
"My siblings should share the responsibility. Why is it on me?"
"This changes things. I owe my parents this time."

text: Three months in, your mother privately says your wife is 'disrespectful.' Your wife says your mother is overbearing. Both want you to say something.

choices.text: "I'd talk to each separately, then bring them together to clear the air."
"I'd ask my wife to be patient — my mother means well."
"I'd ask my mother to give us space — this is between me and my wife."
"I'd avoid taking sides. They need to figure it out themselves."

text: Your parents expect you to live with them after marriage. Your wife wants a separate home. They ask you directly.

choices.text: "We've decided to live separately. We'll visit often."
"Let's try together first — if it doesn't work, we'll move."
"My wife would prefer separate, so I think that's best."
"It's complicated. Let me think about it and let you know."

text: It's the first Eid after marriage. Both families want you for the full day.

choices.text: "We'll split the day — morning with one, evening with the other."
"Wife's family in the morning, my family in the evening. They're closer."
"We'll decide based on what makes the most logistical sense."
"My family always does Eid early — that's the priority, we'll fit hers in after."

text: You're 6 months married. Your wife wants to wait 3-4 years for kids. Your mother keeps asking 'when's the good news?'

choices.text: "It's our timing. I'll tell my mother to stop asking."
"My wife wants to wait. We're respecting that."
"We're not rushing. My mother will adjust."
"There's no rush. When the right time comes, we'll know."

text: Your 6-year-old refuses to pray. Your wife wants gentle persuasion. You were raised with discipline.

choices.text: "She knows what works with him better than I do — I'll follow her lead."
"We need to be consistent. Let's agree on one approach and stick to it."
"Gentle now, but I'd want to step in if he's still refusing in a year."
"I respect her approach, but a father's job is sometimes to be firm."

text: Your child can attend an elite English-medium school (secular, expensive) or a strong Islamic school (conservative, cheaper). Your wife strongly prefers the first.

choices.text: "If she feels strongly, I'd go with her preference."
"Let's visit both before deciding."
"Education matters to her — I'll trust her judgment on this."
"The English-medium school is fine, as long as we balance with religious education at home."

text: You and your wife both work full days. It's 9pm, both tired, dishes are sitting.

choices.text: "I'll do them. You've had a long day."
"Let's split the kitchen — you cook, I clean."
"We should hire help — this is too much for both of us."
"My mother used to handle the kitchen entirely — I'm impressed how she did it."

text: You and your wife have disagreed on a major decision for weeks. Neither will budge.

choices.text: "Let's bring in someone we both trust to hear us out."
"I'll let her have this one — she clearly cares more."
"We need to keep talking until one of us is convinced."
"At some point, someone has to make the call — and that's usually me."

text: Your wife asks for your phone password. You've never shared passwords with anyone.

choices.text: "Of course. Here it is."
"I don't share passwords as a habit — but you can ask to see anything anytime."
"Why? Is something wrong?"
"I'll give it to you, but I'd hope it stays a formality."

text: Both you and your wife earn. How do you set up finances?

choices.text: "Joint account, both have full access to everything."
"Joint for household, separate for personal — privacy matters."
"I'll handle the big stuff, she manages day-to-day expenses."
"Whatever she prefers — I'm flexible."

text: Your unmarried brother needs PKR 5 lakh for a business. You have it in savings accumulated jointly with your wife. Do you tell her?

choices.text: "I'd discuss it with her first — it's our money now."
"I'd tell her, but I'd give the money either way."
"I'd mention it after — it's family, she'd understand."
"She doesn't need to be involved in every family financial decision."

text: Your family hints they expect a significant dowry. Your wife's family is middle-class — providing it would strain them.

choices.text: "I'd tell my family clearly we don't want a dowry."
"I'd talk to my parents privately and ask them to ease off."
"I'd let it play out — my wife's family can give what they can."
"Whatever happens between the families, I'd stay out of it."

text: Your wife prays occasionally, not five times daily. You're consistent. After marriage, this becomes visible.

choices.text: "Her practice is her relationship with Allah. Not my place to push."
"I'd lead by example — hope she joins, but never pressure."
"I'd encourage gently from time to time."
"I'd want the household to maintain a certain standard, and I'd want her with me on that."

text: Your wife's closest friend is non-Muslim. They spend lots of time together — invite her over, go for coffee, etc.

choices.text: "I welcome the friend into our home — diverse friendships are healthy."
"Her friendships are her business. I don't get involved."
"I'm fine with it but quietly hope she also keeps Muslim friends close."
"I'd mention it once that I'd prefer a more religious social circle for her."
